pattern: Counting values in a huge numeric range by a digit-sum property is a job for digit DP over the boundary strings, expressed as a difference of two prefix counts (up to num2, up to num1-1) so the inclusive range collapses into two independent one-sided counts.
hints: Rather than counting the range [num1, num2] directly, build a helper that counts how many values from 0 up to some big-integer bound N have digit sum at most a given cap S -- the range answer falls out of differences of this helper.

count(num1, num2, lo, hi) can be built from four calls to a single 'count values <= N with digit sum <= S' helper: count(<=num2, <=hi) - count(<=num2, <lo) - count(<=num1-1, <=hi) + count(<=num1-1, <lo), all reduced modulo 1e9+7.

Implement the helper as digit DP over N's decimal digits from the most significant digit down, tracking the running digit-sum-so-far and whether the prefix is still tight against N, memoizing on (position, sum-so-far, tight); subtracting one from num1 as a string (careful borrow/leading-zero handling) turns the inclusive lower bound into a strict-upper-bound helper call.

edge_inputs.description: small range spanning multiple digit sums
small range with a tight max_sum
single-value range where num1 equals num2
min_sum equals max_sum, only one exact digit sum qualifies
range crosses a power-of-ten boundary where digit count changes
range excludes everything because max_sum is too small for any value in range
wide sum window that accepts almost every value in a modest range

stress.description: small ranges (kept narrow so the O(range) oracle stays fast) with random sum windows
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    lo = rng.randint(1, 5000)
    width = rng.randint(0, min(4000, size))
    hi = lo + width
    a, b = str(lo), str(hi)
    min_sum = rng.randint(1, 50)
    max_sum = rng.randint(min_sum, min_sum + 50)
    return [a, b, min_sum, max_sum]
